guide_description: One day in Lisbon's oldest and most layered quarter: the Moorish hillside of Alfama climbing to the medieval castle, with the oldest Gothic cathedral in the city at its base and the best viewpoints in Lisbon along the spine.

days.cluster_tagline: Moorish Lisbon on foot - the oldest cathedral, the hilltop castle, and fado country below
days.neighborhood_description: The oldest surviving neighborhood in Lisbon, built on the steep hill east of the Baixa. Alfama was the Moorish medina and survived the 1755 earthquake that leveled most of the city. Its labyrinth of narrow lanes - too tight for Pombaline rebuilding - contains the Romanesque cathedral, the Moorish-founded castle, and a dense concentration of traditional fado houses. The hilltop looks out over the Tagus estuary and the orange rooftops of the lower city.

morning.notes: The oldest church in Lisbon, begun in 1147 on the site of the main Moorish mosque immediately after the Christian reconquest - the military crenellations on the towers are deliberate, not decorative. The Romanesque nave is austere and severe in a way that distinguishes it sharply from the Manueline churches higher on the hill. Enter from the tram-facing west facade and walk through to the Gothic cloister at the rear, where archaeological excavations have exposed Roman, Moorish, and medieval layers simultaneously. Arrive before 10am to beat the 28E tram crowds that stop directly outside.

afternoon.notes: The castle occupies the site of the Moorish alcacova, itself built on Iron Age and Roman fortifications - the layering is the point, not just the walls. Inside the ramparts, the Nucleo Arqueologico contains excavated palace rooms from all periods. Walk the full circuit of the outer walls for unobstructed views from the eastern bastion over the Tagus and from the northern tower across the Baixa grid to the Bairro Alto hill. The peacocks roaming the inner courtyard are not a tourist gimmick - they have lived here since the 16th century.

evening.notes: The least touristed of Lisbon's major miradouros and widely considered by locals to give the best angle on the castle - you see the full profile of the Castelo de Sao Jorge silhouetted against the Tagus rather than looking out from inside it. The kiosk bar opens in late afternoon and serves ginjinha (sour cherry liqueur) and beer to a predominantly local crowd. Come 30 minutes before sunset and stay until the city lights reflect on the river.

if_time_permits.notes: Housed in a 16th-century convent whose chapel is itself covered floor-to-ceiling in blue-and-white azulejo panels, the museum traces Portuguese tile-making from 1500 to the present. The centerpiece is a 23-meter panoramic tile panel depicting Lisbon's waterfront skyline as it looked before the 1755 earthquake - the only detailed visual record of the pre-disaster city. Closed Mondays. A 15-minute walk east of Alfama along the waterfront, or a short Uber.

The terrace above the Alfama rooftops, reached by a short climb from the Santa Luzia viewpoint directly below it. The two terraces are 50 meters apart but Portas do Sol looks slightly north and gives a cleaner angle over the orange rooftops toward the river without the crowd density of Santa Luzia. The 28E tram passes directly below - a good spot to watch it navigate the impossibly narrow lane.
